  • Takeaways

Model Output Plots

Goal

This notebook shows how to visualize simple model output without turning the notebook into a modeling course.

It adapts the clinical-course idea that model results often need plots more than tables.

Analytic Questions

We will ask:

  • How is age associated with systolic blood pressure?
  • How can a fitted line be shown with the observed data?
  • How can predicted values help communicate a model result?

Load Data

nhanes_path <- "data/real/nhanes_2021_2023_analysis.csv"

nhanes <- read_csv(nhanes_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

Prepare Data

adult_bp <- nhanes |>
  filter(
    age_years >= 18,
    !is.na(age_years),
    !is.na(systolic_mean),
    !is.na(gender)
  )

adult_bp |>
  count(gender)
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  gender     n
  <chr>  <int>
1 female  3347
2 male    2776

Data First

p1 <- ggplot(adult_bp, aes(x = age_years, y = systolic_mean)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15)

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Age And Systolic Blood Pressure",
    subtitle = "Points show observations; the line summarizes a linear fit",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Fit A Simple Model

bp_model <- lm(systolic_mean ~ age_years + gender, data = adult_bp)

summary(bp_model)

Call:
lm(formula = systolic_mean ~ age_years + gender, data = adult_bp)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-60.163 -10.494  -1.553   8.431 104.538 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 99.74094    0.66385  150.25   <2e-16 ***
age_years    0.39464    0.01148   34.36   <2e-16 ***
gendermale   4.37547    0.41988   10.42   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 16.36 on 6120 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.1746,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.1743 
F-statistic: 647.3 on 2 and 6120 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Plot Predictions

Predicted values can be easier to read than a coefficient table.

prediction_grid <- expand.grid(
  age_years = seq(20, 80, by = 5),
  gender = sort(unique(adult_bp$gender))
)

predictions <- predict(
  bp_model,
  newdata = prediction_grid,
  interval = "confidence"
)

prediction_data <- bind_cols(
  prediction_grid,
  as.data.frame(predictions)
)

head(prediction_data)
  age_years gender      fit      lwr      upr
1        20 female 107.6338 106.7195 108.5481
2        25 female 109.6070 108.7794 110.4346
3        30 female 111.5802 110.8324 112.3280
4        35 female 113.5534 112.8761 114.2308
5        40 female 115.5267 114.9071 116.1462
6        45 female 117.4999 116.9218 118.0779
p1 <- ggplot(prediction_data, aes(x = age_years, y = fit, color = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lwr, ymax = upr), alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1)

p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Systolic Blood Pressure By Age And Gender",
    subtitle = "Predictions come from a simple linear model fit to adult NHANES observations",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Predicted mean systolic blood pressure",
    color = "Gender",
    fill = "Gender"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Put Observed Data In The Background

p1 <- ggplot(adult_bp, aes(x = age_years, y = systolic_mean)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, color = "gray40") +
  geom_line(
    data = prediction_data,
    aes(x = age_years, y = fit, color = gender),
    linewidth = 1
  )

p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Model Summaries Should Stay Connected To The Data",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Mean systolic blood pressure",
    color = "Gender"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Takeaways

  • Plot the data before plotting the model.
  • Model plots are summaries of assumptions and fitted patterns.
  • Prediction plots can be easier to interpret than coefficient tables.
  • Keep model summaries connected to the data they summarize.
